title: Clinical Biochemistry Scientist
how_to_become: To become a clinical biochemistry scientist, complete an undergraduate honours degree, gain postgraduate research experience, and complete the NHS Scientist Training Programme (STP). This typically takes 8 to 11 years in total.

salary.entry_note: Trainee clinical scientists start at £41,608 before tax.
salary.experienced_note: Qualified clinical scientists can earn up to £59,159. Principal clinical scientists up to £79,278. Consultant clinical scientists up to £128,051 before tax.

description: Clinical biochemistry scientists are highly skilled healthcare professionals who investigate the chemical composition of blood and other body fluids. They have a vital role in the diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment of a wide range of diseases from diabetes to cancer.
key_responsibilities: Diagnostic analysis — performing and interpreting complex biochemical tests using advanced techniques such as mass spectrometry.
Clinical advice — acting as a consultant to doctors, nurses, and other healthcare professionals on diagnosis and management of conditions like kidney failure, hormone imbalances, and cardiac issues.
Service leadership — managing laboratory sections, staff training, and quality assurance to meet rigorous accreditation standards.
Research and innovation — developing new diagnostic biomarkers and validating new tests for earlier disease detection.

entry_requirements.overview: An undergraduate honours degree at SCQF level 10 in a relevant subject is required, followed by postgraduate study and the NHS STP.

work_experience: Shadowing the Clinical Biochemistry department at a local hospital, placements during university, and open day visits are recommended to strengthen applications.

widening_access_routes: Degree Link — guaranteed progression from HNC/HND to Robert Gordon University.
Focus West — programme for S4-S6 pupils from lower-income or underrepresented backgrounds.
Scottish Wider Access Programme (SWAP) — one-year college courses providing a route to higher education.
postgraduate_options: Master of Science (MSc) — 1 year, taught degree with research project.
Master of Research (MRes) — 1 year, independent study focused on a large research project.
Doctor of Philosophy (PhD) — 3 to 4 years, original research.
education_and_training.training_programme: NHS Scientist Training Programme (STP) — 3-year fixed-term postgraduate training combining workplace learning with a master's degree in Clinical Science (Biochemistry). Includes rotation through blood science specialties, block-release study at a partner university, and practical experience in NHS Scotland laboratories.
education_and_training.timeline: Approximately 8 to 11 years: 4 years for honours degree, 1 to 4 years for postgraduate study, 3 years for STP.
education_and_training.equivalence_route: Experienced scientists can apply for registration via the equivalence route through AHCS or ACS, involving a portfolio submission and viva voce assessment.

learning_and_development.overview: Clinical scientists must undertake CPD to keep knowledge and skills up to date and maintain HCPC registration.
further_qualifications: Fellowship of the Royal College of Pathologists (FRCPath)

professional_bodies: Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC) — mandatory for NHS practice

chunks.text: To become a clinical biochemistry scientist, complete an undergraduate honours degree, postgraduate study (MSc, MRes, or PhD), and the 3-year NHS Scientist Training Programme (STP). The full pathway typically takes 8 to 11 years.

Trainee clinical scientists start at £41,608 before tax. Qualified clinical scientists earn up to £59,159. Principal clinical scientists up to £79,278. Consultant clinical scientists up to £128,051 before tax.
Clinical biochemistry scientists investigate the chemical composition of blood and body fluids, playing a vital role in diagnosing, monitoring, and treating diseases from diabetes to cancer.

Clinical biochemistry scientists perform and interpret complex biochemical tests, provide clinical advice to doctors and nurses, manage laboratory services and quality assurance, and develop new diagnostic biomarkers and tests.

Useful school subjects include Chemistry, Biology, Maths, Physics, and English. An undergraduate honours degree in Biochemistry, Biomedical Sciences, Chemistry, or Molecular Biology at SCQF level 10 is required.
Shadowing the Clinical Biochemistry department at a local hospital, university placements, and open day visits are recommended to strengthen applications.

After an honours degree, postgraduate study (MSc, MRes, or PhD) is required. The NHS STP is a 3-year programme combining workplace learning with a master's degree in Clinical Science (Biochemistry). Widening access routes include Degree Link, Focus West, and SWAP. An equivalence route is available for experienced scientists.
Clinical biochemistry scientists must maintain CPD and HCPC registration. Senior roles often involve the Fellowship of the Royal College of Pathologists (FRCPath).

With experience and qualifications, clinical biochemistry scientists can progress to principal clinical scientist and consultant clinical scientist roles involving clinical responsibility, strategic management, and leading research programmes.

Clinical biochemistry scientists must register with the HCPC. They can also join the IBMS, Academy for Healthcare Science (AHCS), Association of Clinical Scientists (ACS), and Royal College of Pathologists.
